macro_segments.title: Blemished Animals and Non-Altar Sanctity: Why They Cannot Make an Exchange (Temura)

micro_segments.title: Introduction: An animal with a blemish before sanctification never receives altar sanctity, only money-sanctity
The prohibition of exchange (Temura) from the verse: only animals that originally were unblemished can make an exchange
Distinguishing between animals that started good (unblemished) versus started bad (blemished): only the former creates altar sanctity capable of exchange

macro_segments.title: Redemption of Sanctified Animals: When Must They Be Alive?

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Shimon's position: altar-sanctified animals need to be alive for redemption; treasure-sanctified animals do not
The Tana of the House of Levi's dissent: all sanctified animals, regardless of purpose, must be alive for redemption
Attempted reconciliation: Whether the Sages who dispute Rabbi Shimon agree on the same principle or reverse it
Two separate reasons an animal must be alive: the biblical requirement of standing and valuation; and the rabbinic concern not to redeem sanctified items for dog food

macro_segments.title: Residual Sanctity After Redemption: Exemption from Firstborn and Priestly Gifts

micro_segments.title: The comparison to deer and hart proves exemption from firstborn; the word hart extends this to exemption from priestly gifts
Debate over whether the comparison to wild animals goes too far: can atonement-restrictions apply?
Rava's reinterpretation: the word 'but' excludes only mother-and-offspring; the blood prohibition teaches that fat carries the same stringency as blood

macro_segments.title: Restrictions After Redemption: No Work, Shearing, or Milking Permitted

micro_segments.title: The verse 'slaughter and eat' proves you cannot work, shear, or milk a redeemed blemished animal; only slaughter and eating are permitted
Alternative reading: the permitted benefit is only after slaughter; before slaughter all benefit is forbidden
Biblical basis for the principle: you cannot redeem sanctified items destined for dog food

macro_segments.title: Offspring and Milk of Redeemed Animals: Forbidden Due to Residual Sanctity

micro_segments.title: Question: Why are offspring and milk forbidden even after redemption if the animal is compared to a wild animal?
Answer: Only offspring born after redemption retain the mother's sanctity if conceived before redemption
Full sanctity for offspring born before redemption: they can be brought as a sacrifice (though some opinions dispute this)

macro_segments.title: Offspring Born After Redemption: Derailed Sanctity and Special Treatment

micro_segments.title: Rav Kahuna's position: offspring born after the mother's redemption have derailed sanctity and must be locked in a room to die
Alternative solution: sanctify the offspring in utero for the mother's intended sacrifice before redeeming the mother, avoiding derailed sanctity
Ravina's question and Rav Sheshet's answer: you cannot sanctify offspring for a different type of sacrifice, just as you cannot add sanctity to a firstborn

macro_segments.title: Comprehensive Review: Blemished-From-Start vs. Blemished-After-Sanctification in the Baraita

micro_segments.title: Case 1: Blemish before sanctification—animal is treated as ordinary after redemption; no firstborn or gift obligations; offspring are completely de-sanctified
Case 2: Sanctification then blemish—animal retains residual sanctity; exempt from firstborn and gifts; restrictions on work, shearing, milking; offspring remain sanctified
Final principle: animals with residual sanctity remain wholly sanctified in all respects; the only permitted use after redemption is eating

topical_tags.term: Tana d'Bei Levi (House of Levi tradition)
Podim et hakodashim l'ha'achilan l'klavim (redeeming for dog food)
